text:
High among top improbable feats in track and field, up near the border of impossible, is a combination of gold medals many elite sprinters wouldn芒聙聶t dare attempt. On the surface, it sounds simple. Win gold in the 100 meters. Then win gold in the 200 meters.

And yet, only nine men have completed The Double in the history of the Olympics. More than half won completed their double by 1936芒聙聰and eight of nine did so before by the turn of this century. Usain Bolt doubled at Beijing 2008, London 2012 and Rio 2016. (On the women芒聙聶s side, seven competitors in Olympic history have completed doubles. Only one female sprinter has doubled this century芒聙聰Elaine Thompson-Herah, who did it twice in 2012 and 芒聙聵16.脗

As far as U.S. sprinters, the last man and woman to complete this particular double are both icons from the 1980s. Carl Lewis completed his double in 1984; Florence Griffith-Joyner, in 芒聙聶88. All of which makes Noah Lyles芒聙聶 upcoming schedule that much more intriguing. He won both events at the last world championships, although that芒聙聶s not exactly an even comparison for what芒聙聶s ahead.脗

To even attempt such insanity says something about Lyles, about the way he has always been, since birth. At first, he just ran. Then, he ran fast. Then, he began noticing Olympians芒聙聰Jearl Miles, Joetta Clark, Hazel Clark芒聙聰who were also family friends. He didn芒聙聶t know it then, but his attempt at The Double had been set in motion.

He didn芒聙聶t decide to make a literal sprint toward the Olympics until watching the opening ceremony in London in 2012芒聙聰that altered how he viewed the Games and their significance. He decided to make the next U.S. national team, ahead of Rio, but fell short. He qualified for the Tokyo Games while dealing with depression and anxiety.

His coach, Lance Brauman, knew doubling was possible from the first time they met. Lyles already ranked among the strongest 200-meter runners in his age group, then in the world. But he had also won a world junior championship in the 100, which meant, in theory, he could do the same thing at higher levels, too. Brauman says Lyles needed to get stronger and more physical in order to handle the rounds required of any aspiring double-r.脗

They trained for that. At the world championships in 2022, Lyles clocked a 19.31-seconds 200-meter dash, only 0.12 off Bolt芒聙聶s world mark. Right around then, his dream of doubling rounded into view. At the 芒聙聶23 world championships, Netflix film crew in tow, he decided to attempt the double and see how it went. Pretty well, it turns out; he won both.脗

芒聙聹That put him in a totally different conversation,芒聙聺 says Lyles芒聙聶 mother, Keisha Bishop.

But the world championships don芒聙聶t always include all of the best athletes. At the Olympics, the field features nearly every top competitor, at or near peak performance.脗聽脗

The double might appear simple: run fast at the shortest Olympic distance and win; double the length and win again. But there芒聙聶s so many factors and variables beyond the surface glance. Lyles needed to maintain his strength to muscle through 100-meter finals. But adding too much muscle could slow him in both races. Lyles would need to be fast, but in different ways, which would require tweaks to his diet, workouts and rest schedule. He needed to prepare his body for six rounds in the 100 and the 200, plus any additional relays he might run.

芒聙聹Most people have no idea what goes into it,芒聙聺 Brauman says.

As for specific adjustments to his regimen, Brauman politely declines to share any relevant details. But he does cite major gains in Lyles芒聙聶 cerebral approach to sprinting. 芒聙聹He can make changes that some athletes aren芒聙聶t able to [make],芒聙聺 Brauman says. 芒聙聹Just feeling different parts of his body and where they芒聙聶re at in space at times, and being proprioceptively aware of how different segments of his body are moving, in order to fire patterns of muscles and everything else in that way.

芒聙聹It芒聙聶s a combination of everything. But that芒聙聶s part of it. He芒聙聶s a couple years older and has a better understanding of the sport and who he is.芒聙聺

Four-time Olympic gold medalist Michael Johnson loves everything about Lyles. He saysLyles芒聙聶 colorful personalityreminds him of a former teammate, Maurice Greene.脗

芒聙聹Noah, he can back it up,芒聙聺 Johnson says. 芒聙聹He芒聙聶s literally unafraid to have the spotlight on him, but [he will] also go out there and perform.芒聙聺

Lyles did just that in Eugene, Ore., at the U.S. Olympic trials. Now, he must do the same thing against the world芒聙聶s best sprinters in Paris芒聙聰on the double.

Greg Bishop is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated who has covered every kind of sport and every major event across six continents for more than two decades. He previously worked for The Seattle Times and The New York Times. He is the co-author of two books: Jim Gray芒聙聶s memoir, 芒聙聹Talking to GOATs芒聙聺; and Laurent Duvernay Tardif芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Red Zone芒聙聺. Bishop has written for Showtime Sports, Prime Video and DAZN, and has been nominated for eight sports Emmys, winning two, both for production. He has completed more than a dozen documentary film projects, with a wide range of duties. Bishop, who graduated from the Newhouse School at Syracuse University, is based in Seattle.

title: Noah Lyles Chases Usain Bolt and the Sprint Double at the Paris Olympics
description: The 27-year-old will attempt gold in the 100 and 200 meter races at the 2024 Games, a feat last completed on the men芒聙聶s side by the Jamaican star in 2008, 芒聙聵12 and 芒聙聵16.
